article_title: Marimo Python Notebook Pre-Auth RCE Weaponized 9 Hours After Disclosure as CISA Adds CVE to KEV Catalog

content_preview.title: Marimo Python Notebook Pre-Auth RCE Weaponized 9 Hours After Disclosure as CISA Adds CVE to KEV Catalog
content_preview.summary: An unauthenticated WebSocket flaw in the popular Marimo notebook (CVE-2026-39987, CVSS 9.3) was weaponized within 9 hours 41 minutes of disclosure, with credential theft completed in under three minutes. CISA has since added the bug to its KEV catalog with a May 7 federal deadline.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A critical pre-authentication remote code execution flaw in Marimo, a popular open-source Python notebook used by data scientists and machine-learning practitioners, was weaponized in the wild less than ten hours after its public disclosure, illustrating how rapidly modern attackers can build working exploits from advisory text alone. The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency has [added the bug to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog](https://www.cisa.gov/know...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Bot disputed both round-1 findings with primary-source evidence and was correct on both counts. Both findings withdrawn after independent verification.

finding_1_cisa_kev_narrative.round_1_finding: Fabricated CISA/KEV/May-7 federal-deadline narrative — claimed not present in any cited source.
finding_1_cisa_kev_narrative.bot_response: Disputed. The narrative is real; the round-1 issue was misattribution to CSO Online (which doesn't mention CISA). Bot added CISA's KEV catalog directly as a primary source.
finding_1_cisa_kev_narrative.reviewer_verification: VERIFIED. CISA KEV catalog snapshot (source-3) lists CVE-2026-39987 with 'Date Added: 2026-04-23' and 'Due Date: 2026-05-07' verbatim, plus the BOD 22-01 required-action language quoted in the article. The article's claim is accurate; the round-1 fabrication finding was based on the wrong source attribution rather than a fabricated fact.

finding_2_patch_date.round_1_finding: Patch date misstated as April 9 — claimed BleepingComputer's 'yesterday relative to April 12' implied April 11.
finding_2_patch_date.bot_response: Disputed. The April 9 date is correct per the primary source: GitHub release tag for Marimo 0.23.0. Bot added the GitHub release URL as a direct source.
finding_2_patch_date.reviewer_verification: VERIFIED. GitHub release tag snapshot (source-5) carries datetime="2026-04-09T14:00:52Z" — confirming the patch was published April 9, 2026 at 14:00:52 UTC, exactly as the article states. My round-1 inference from BleepingComputer's loose 'yesterday' framing was wrong.

source_verification.https://www.cisa.gov/known-exploited-vul: source-3.html — CVE-2026-39987 entry verified: Date Added 2026-04-23, Due Date 2026-05-07, required action 'Apply mitigations per vendor instructions, follow applicable BOD 22-01 guidance for cloud services, or discontinue use of the product if mitigations are unavailable.' All match the article verbatim.
source_verification.https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-202: source-4.html — verified: CVE-2026-39987 listed with CVSS 4.0 base score 9.3 CRITICAL.
source_verification.https://github.com/marimo-team/marimo/re: source-5.html — verified: release datetime 2026-04-09T14:00:52Z, confirming the April 9 patch claim.
source_verification.round_1_sources: SecurityWeek, BleepingComputer, CSO Online — all verified verbatim in round 1 for the technical content (CVE ID, primitive, 9h41m exploitation, /terminal/ws, validate_auth(), Sysdig honeypot, 125-IP scanning, 20K GitHub stars, .env/SSH/AWS credential theft, April 8 disclosure date).

editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims now verified across primary sources (CISA, NVD, GitHub release) and secondary reporting (SecurityWeek, BleepingComputer, CSO Online).
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, technical. Headline appropriately leads with the 9h41m weaponization window and includes the CISA/KEV framing now that it's directly sourced.
recommendations: Future round-1 reviews on cybersecurity articles: when an article cites 'federal deadline' / 'KEV' / 'CISA' content not present in the named secondary source, check the primary CISA KEV catalog before flagging as fabricated. The bot's editorial judgment in this rewrite was sound.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. Both round-1 findings withdrawn after primary-source verification. The bot expanded sources from 3 to 6 and correctly traced every previously-flagged claim back to its actual primary source. This is the right journalistic outcome.
